1: A direct revelation: Jesus Christ the righteous as our advocate (parakletos) before the Father. The intercession of Christ at the right hand of the Father is the NT's central heavenly-ministry claim — here stated in the pastoral context of community members who sin. The righteous one advocates for the unrighteous; his qualification is not his community membership but his character (dikaios) and his completed atonement (1 John 2:2). This is not typological but direct: no OT high priest is needed as the comparison point; Christ himself is named as the present, effective advocate.
2: A direct revelation: he is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but for the whole world. The Christological office of hilasmos (propitiation) belongs to Christ directly — not prefigured by the mercy seat (though the typological connection enriches the meaning) but exercised by Christ as his own completed act. The scope (the whole world) is the universal offer that the community possesses as its own experience and proclaims to all others. No further mediation is needed: Christ is the complete and sufficient atoning sacrifice for all.
22: A direct revelation: whoever denies that Jesus is the Christ denies the Father also. The Christological criterion here is not ethical but confessional: the content of faith determines the reality of one's relationship to the Father. This is not rationalism (the right formula saves) but the recognition that the Father has identified himself definitively through the Son — to refuse the Son is to refuse the Father's own self-disclosure. The mutual-indwelling theology (Son and Father) means that acceptance or rejection of Christ is acceptance or rejection of the God who sent him.
3: A revelation of God: knowing Christ produces obedience to his commands, because the one who genuinely knows Christ knows the God who is light and love — and that knowledge necessarily reshapes conduct. The knowledge of Christ is not merely intellectual assent (the Gnostic/Cerinthian claim) but covenantal participation: to know him is to live in his life, and his life is characterized by obedience to the Father (John 15:10). The community tests its own Christological claims by this ethical criterion — knowledge without obedience is not knowledge of the Christ who obeyed perfectly.
4: A revelation of God: the negative test of genuine Christological knowledge is disobedience. Knowing Christ — who is himself the truth (John 14:6) — cannot coexist with untruthful living. The "truth not in that person" is not merely moral deficiency but a statement about Christ's absence: Christ (the truth) is not actually present in the person whose life contradicts his character. The liar characterization is epistemological, not merely moral — claiming to know the truthful one while living falsely is a self-refuting impossibility.
5: A revelation of God: obedience to Christ's word is the context in which love for God reaches its completion (teleiōtai). The entire movement of the incarnation is toward the formation of a community in which love is fully expressed (John 15:9-12). Christ's word is the instrument of that formation, and the obedient community is the evidence of its effectiveness — love made complete in obedient persons is the visible result of Christ's incarnation and teaching. To be in him is to have one's love brought to its goal through his word's indwelling.
6: A direct revelation: the ethical criterion is biographical — the incarnate life of Jesus is the pattern for the community's life. This is an extraordinary claim: the historical life of Jesus of Nazareth (his walking, his serving, his self-giving love) is the norm for all who claim to be "in him." The incarnation is not merely salvific but exemplary — and the example is not a principle but a person whose specific way of living is the template for the community. The Docetic Christ who did not truly live a human life cannot serve as this biographical standard.
7: A theme: the love command is old — rooted in Leviticus 19:18 and in the community's founding teaching from Jesus (John 13:34-35). The antiquity of the command connects the community to the entire history of God's covenant love-demand from Sinai forward. Christ did not replace the love command but fulfilled and radicalized it: the "old command" is now understood in the light of the love Christ demonstrated at the cross, revealing for the first time the full depth of what "love your neighbor" means when the neighbor is loved to the point of death.
8: A direct revelation: the new command's truth is "seen in him" — in the incarnate, crucified, and risen Christ. The love that the command requires is no longer merely commanded but modeled and made possible by Christ: the darkness is passing because Christ has defeated the prince of darkness, and the true light is already shining because Christ is the light of the world (John 8:12). The community in which the command is being obeyed is the visible evidence that the new age Christ inaugurated has arrived in their midst.
9: A revelation of God: the light that Christ revealed (1 John 1:5) is not merely conceptual but ethical-relational. Hating a brother while claiming to be in Christ (who is the light) is a contradiction because the one who is the light is also the one who commanded love (John 13:34). The false claim to light-dwelling while practicing hatred is a Christological inconsistency: you cannot be in the one who is light while walking in the darkness that he came to dispel. The community's love is the evidence of its light-dwelling; its hatred would be the evidence of its darkness.
10: A revelation of God: the positive verification of light-dwelling is love for the community. The community formed by Christ's love becomes light-dwelling by practicing his love among themselves. The stumbling-block absence points to the community's integrity — those who genuinely love are not morally self-contradicting and therefore neither stumble themselves nor cause others to stumble. Christ himself said "I am the light of the world; whoever follows me will not walk in darkness" (John 8:12) — love for the community is the form that following Christ takes in the community's life together.
11: A revelation of God: hatred and its consequent darkness-walking produces disorientation — the one who hates does not know where they are going. Christ who is the light said "whoever follows me will have the light of life" (John 8:12); to hate the community is to abandon the following that gives orientation. The secessionists' confident claim to spiritual advancement is, on this analysis, a disoriented wandering in the dark — they have left the light-community and now walk without the orientation that the light of Christ provides.
12: A direct revelation: the entire ethical exhortation of the letter rests on the prior, foundational reality of forgiveness through Christ's name. The community does not pursue ethical faithfulness to earn forgiveness but because forgiveness is already given in Christ. The ethical confidence of the letter (you can walk in the light; you can keep the commands) is rooted in this accomplished forensic reality: the sins are forgiven, and the community stands accepted in Christ — which is both the motivation for and the ground of the ethical life the letter calls them to.
13: A direct revelation: knowing "him who is from the beginning" is knowing the preexistent Christ — the eternal Son who was before all things (John 1:1; 1 John 1:1). The deep knowing of the mature community members is specifically Christological: they have known the one whose origin precedes history, the eternal life that appeared to them (1 John 1:2). This knowledge is not speculative theology but the experiential testimony of those who encountered the one who was from the beginning in the historical Jesus — the ancient and eternal one made flesh.
14: A direct revelation: the indwelling word of God that makes the young men strong against the evil one is Christ himself — the Word (John 1:1) who dwells in those who receive him (John 1:12-13). The victory over the evil one by the community is grounded in the stronger one (Christ) who has already overcome the world (John 16:33) and whose word abides in the community as their living resource. The community is not self-sufficient against the evil one; their strength is derivative — the indwelling word of the one who has already won the victory.
15: A revelation of God: the exclusive love-claim of the Father (mediated through Christ) means the world cannot be an additional object of love without displacing the Father. Christ made the same exclusive claim: "No one can serve two masters" (Matthew 6:24). Love for the world is not supplemented by love for the Father — it crowds it out, because the world and the Father make rival totality-claims on the community's allegiance. The community that has received God's love in Christ is claimed entirely by that love, which leaves no room for the world's rival claim.
16: A revelation of God: the threefold taxonomy of worldly desire (lust of the flesh, lust of the eyes, pride of life) reveals the character of what is not from the Father. The diagnosis is Christologically grounded: Christ entered the world and was not captured by these desires — tempted in all points as we are, yet without sin (Hebrews 4:15). The community in Christ is called to the same freedom from world-orientation that Christ demonstrated in his incarnate life: the desires that organized the world around the creation rather than the Creator have been refused by the one in whom the community lives.
17: A direct revelation: the permanence is in the will of God enacted in Christ. "Whoever does the will of God lives forever" is the community's participation in the eternal life that Christ is (1 John 1:2) and gives (1 John 5:11-12). The world's transience is the Christological truth revealed by the resurrection: what the world called permanent (its values, structures, desires) has been shown to be passing; what the world called weak and defeated (the crucified Christ) has been shown to be the only permanent thing — the eternal life of the Father given in the Son, which no worldly power can extinguish.
18: A direct revelation: the "last hour" is the eschatological period inaugurated by Christ's coming — the time in which the forces of anti-Christ are active precisely because Christ has come and claimed the world as his own. The many antichrists are the sign that the Antichrist's spirit is already present and active, but their very existence confirms that the last hour has arrived: Christ's coming has triggered the Christological crisis expected in the end times (Matthew 24:4-5, 11, 24), making the community's crisis an eschatological event, not merely a local dispute.
19: A direct revelation: the departure of the secessionists is interpreted through the Christological lens of remaining/abiding. Christ commanded his disciples to "remain in me" (John 15:4-10); those who are genuinely in Christ remain in the community that confesses him. The departure reveals that the secessionists were not genuinely in Christ — their Christology was defective from the beginning, making their apparent community membership a surface affiliation rather than genuine union with the Son. Their going is the revelation of what was always the case: they were not of us.
20: A direct revelation: the anointing (chrisma) from the Holy One is the community's share in Christ's own anointing — he is the Christ (the anointed one), and those who are in him share his anointing by the Spirit. The community's knowledge of the truth rests not on their individual spiritual achievement but on the Spirit's indwelling that Christ promised (John 14:26; 1 John 15:26). The anointing is democratizing: every member of the community has it, not merely the spiritually advanced — a direct counter to any two-tier Christology that reserves genuine access to Christ for an enlightened elite.
21: A revelation of God: the incompatibility of lie and truth is rooted in the character of Christ who is the truth (John 14:6). If the truth (Christ) genuinely dwells in the community, no lie can coexist with it — they are ontologically incompatible. The pastoral assurance (they know the truth) is simultaneously the Christological claim: the one who is the truth is genuinely present in the community, making their knowledge of the truth a participation in the person of Christ rather than a merely cognitive attainment. They know the truth because they are in the one who is the truth.
23: A direct revelation: no one who denies the Son has the Father; whoever acknowledges the Son has the Father also. The Son-Father unity is the positive side of the same exclusive Christological claim: acknowledging the Son is the condition of having the Father, because the Father has identified himself definitively through the Son and there is no other way to him. The secessionists who redefine the Son or separate the divine Christ from the human Jesus have denied the one gateway to the Father that the Father himself has established — making their claimed possession of the Father impossible.
24: A direct revelation: the community's abiding in the Son and in the Father is conditioned on the original apostolic proclamation about the historical Jesus remaining in them. The mutual abiding of the community in the Father and Son is not a mystical achievement but a consequence of doctrinal fidelity: hold the original testimony about who Jesus is (the Christ, the Son of God, who came in the flesh), and you hold the condition for the Trinitarian fellowship the letter describes. The departure from the original Christology is a departure from the very condition for abiding in the Son.
25: A direct revelation: the promise of eternal life is specifically Christ's promise — "he" refers to the Son who is eternal life (1 John 1:2) and who promised it to those who believe in him (John 3:16; 1 John 10:28; 1 John 11:25-26). The community holds not merely a doctrine about eternal life but the promise of the one who is himself the eternal life. Their hope is not in a destiny but in a person — the Son who promised and who keeps his promises because his life is indestructible. The promise grounds the community's perseverance: what they hold is the word of the one who is life itself.
26: A revelation of God: the warning context is Christological — the leading-astray is specifically about the false Christology of the secessionists. The pastoral act of writing is itself Christologically motivated: the letter protects the community's hold on the genuine Christ against the alternative christ who cannot save. Writing to warn about false Christology is the apostolic equivalent of the Spirit's role as the helper who leads into all truth (John 16:13) — the apostle and the Spirit together guard the community's access to the genuine Christ.
27: A direct revelation: the Spirit's teaching-anointing that remains in the community is Christ's own promised provision — the Paraclete whom he said would teach all things (John 14:26; 1 John 16:13). The community's self-sufficiency in relation to the secessionists' claimed teaching authority rests on Christ's own promise: the Spirit he sent makes the community capable of discerning truth from error. They do not need the secessionists because they have the Spirit of the one who is the truth — and that Spirit teaches them all that they need to abide in Christ.
28: A direct revelation: the parousia of Christ — "when he appears" — is the Christological horizon that grounds the letter's exhortation to abide in him. Christ will appear (phanerōthē) — the same verb used of his first appearing (1 John 3:2, 5, 8; 1 John 4:9) — making the second coming the completion of the same revelation that the incarnation began. The community's confidence at his appearing is the Christological test: those who have genuinely remained in the Christ who will appear will not be ashamed when he appears, because they will recognize in the appearing one the same one in whom they have abided throughout.
29: A revelation of God: Christ's righteousness (dikaios) is the standard by which the community's conduct is assessed. Born of him, the children bear the character of their divine Father — specifically the righteousness that is essential to Christ's character. This is the Christological basis of the community's ethical life: not imitation of an external standard but reproduction of an internal nature communicated in new birth. Those born of God produce righteousness because they share in the nature of the righteous one — the divine character transmitted generatively through the new birth.
